meta.audience: MBA students with zero computer-science background
meta.tone: basic concepts only, no code required. RunG 1-3: all options plausible. RUNG 4-7: exactly ONE funny/absurd distractor, the other two wrong options are normal/plausible (so 50-50 still strips a real wrong answer).
meta.source: AIHR article 'HR Data Analytics: 5 Python Libraries You Need in HR' + the 16-slide teaching deck
meta.schema: id, library, difficulty(1-7), question, options[4], correct_index, explanation (code omitted on purpose: audience is non-technical)
meta.note: Seed bank. Expand to 4-5 per tier for richer rotation. draw_round() picks exactly one per tier, so difficulty always ramps 1..7. Correct answer is always options[0].

questions.question: This whole course is about learning one programming language for HR data. Which one?
questions.explanation: Python is the language. It is free, readable, and backed by libraries that handle the core HR analytics tasks, from cleaning data to predicting who might leave.

questions.question: In this game, what do you win for each correct answer instead of cash?
options: A promotion to a higher HR role
questions.explanation: Instead of cash, you climb the HR ladder: each right answer promotes you, from HR Intern at the bottom up to CHRO at the top.

questions.question: Python is described as 'free and open source.' What does that mean in practice?
options: Anyone can use and study it without paying
It is owned by one company that charges per use
It can only run inside Excel
You need an Aadhar card to install it
questions.explanation: Open source means the code is public: anyone can use, read, and improve it for free. That is why these five HR libraries cost nothing.

questions.question: Pandas is nicknamed 'The Organizer.' What is its main job?
options: Tidy, clean, and sort messy HR data
It designs the company org chart
questions.explanation: Pandas loads a messy HRIS or payroll export and lets you clean blanks, fix duplicates, and group data, like a smarter spreadsheet you command with words.

questions.question: Before Python, where do many HR analysts start their data work?
questions.explanation: Excel is a common starting point. It works for simple reports, but the course shows where it taps out and why Python helps.

questions.question: An HRIS or payroll export usually lands on your desk as what kind of data?
options: A table of rows and columns
A live video feed of the office
questions.explanation: HR systems export tables: rows of employees, columns of attributes like department, salary, and attrition. Pandas is built to work with exactly this.

questions.question: NumPy is 'The Calculator.' It is the math engine that sits underneath which other library?
questions.explanation: Pandas is built on top of NumPy. When you run a Pandas calculation, NumPy is often doing the math underneath, fast and reliably.

questions.question: The course mnemonic is 'clean, calculate, show, check, predict.' Which library is 'show'?
questions.explanation: Seaborn is 'The Illustrator' and maps to 'show.' It turns workforce data into charts leaders can actually read.

questions.question: Which library is 'The Illustrator' that helps you build charts for a CFO or CHRO?
questions.explanation: Seaborn is the charting library. Analysis only creates value when leaders understand it, and Seaborn makes your findings boardroom-ready.

questions.question: Statsmodels is 'The Auditor.' What does it give you to prove a finding is real?
questions.explanation: Statsmodels produces p-values. A p-value below 0.05 says a pattern is unlikely to be chance alone, turning 'looks like a gap' into 'this is a gap.'

questions.question: In Statsmodels, a p-value below 0.05 tells you what?
options: The pattern is unlikely to be random chance
The sample size was large enough
questions.explanation: Below 0.05 means the result would be very unusual if nothing were really happening. That is the bar for calling a finding real.

questions.question: Scikit-learn is 'The Forecaster.' What is its headline job?
options: Predict what may happen next, like who might leave
questions.explanation: Scikit-learn builds predictive models. It moves HR from 'turnover was 18% last year' to 'these employees may be at higher flight risk next quarter.'

questions.question: Before trusting a pay-equity finding, 'The Auditor' checks the gap after controlling for what?
options: Fair factors like tenure and education
questions.explanation: A real pay-equity test asks: does a salary gap remain after fair factors like tenure and education are counted? If it does, the gap is worth investigating.

questions.question: After you clean data (Pandas) and do the math (NumPy), what comes next in the workflow?
options: Save it to the HR drive
questions.explanation: The order is clean, calculate, show, check, predict. Once the numbers are ready, Seaborn turns them into charts people can read.

questions.question: In a flight-risk model, what is 'feature importance'?
options: A ranked list of what drives attrition most
The average weight of the office
The cost of each open role
questions.explanation: Feature importance ranks which factors matter most in the model. It is boardroom gold because it shows which levers actually drive attrition.

questions.question: Why does the course say Seaborn matters so much?
options: It transforms complex data into publication-ready statistical graphics
It reduces the cost of Rapido bookings
It replaces the whole HR team
questions.explanation: The deck's line: 'Findings that nobody understands change nothing.' Seaborn is how your analysis earns its meeting slot with leaders.

questions.question: Scikit-learn moves HR analytics from reporting the past to what?
questions.explanation: Prediction is the point. From describing what happened to forecasting who may leave, HR shifts from historian to strategist.

questions.question: To check a model honestly, you split data into training and test sets. Why bother?
options: To test the model on data it never saw while learning
To send the model to Olympics
To keep a backup copy of the file
questions.explanation: Holding out test data the model never trained on shows whether it genuinely learned or just memorized. That is honest validation.

questions.question: A pay-equity regression shows a gender pay gap with a p-value of 0.20 after controlling for tenure and education. What is the most defensible reading?
options: The remaining gap is not statistically significant, so it could be chance
The gap is proven real and legally actionable
Gender has no effect on pay anywhere in the company
The model is broken and must be discarded
questions.explanation: A p-value of 0.20 is above the 0.05 bar, so the leftover gap is not statistically significant: it may be chance. That is different from proving no gap exists, and different from a broken model.

questions.question: Your attrition model scores 95% accuracy, but only 5% of employees actually left. Why might that number be misleading?
options: A model that always predicts 'stays' would also score about 95%
95% accuracy is always excellent for HR use
Higher accuracy always means better predictions of leavers
Accuracy cannot be measured on imbalanced data
questions.explanation: When only 5% leave, predicting 'nobody leaves' is right 95% of the time yet useless. On imbalanced data, accuracy hides poor detection of the rare group, which is exactly who HR cares about.

questions.question: You must both confirm a pay gap is statistically real AND predict who is likely to leave next quarter. Which pairing of libraries fits?
options: Statsmodels to confirm the gap, Scikit-learn to predict leavers
Seaborn to confirm the gap, Pandas to predict leavers
NumPy to confirm the gap, Seaborn to predict leavers
Pandas to confirm the gap, NumPy to predict leavers
questions.explanation: Confirming significance is 'check' (Statsmodels); forecasting who leaves is 'predict' (Scikit-learn). The others are cleaning, math, or charting tools, not testing or prediction.
